---
title: "How LLMs work"
subtitle: "Just enough to reason about their behavior in research"
---

::: {.callout-note}
## Learning objectives
- Explain, at a working level, what an LLM is doing when it generates text.
- Distinguish pretraining, post-training (RLHF / preference tuning), and inference.
- Connect mechanism to behavior: why LLMs hallucinate, why context matters, why they sometimes refuse.
:::

## What an LLM is

*TODO: concise explainer — next-token prediction, transformer architecture at a functional level, tokenization, context windows, temperature.*

Key takeaways for a working researcher:

- An LLM is a function from *token sequence* to *probability distribution over next tokens*.
- The model has no internal representation of "what is true" — only "what is likely given the training distribution."
- Long contexts are not memory — they're the only thing the model sees.

## Pretraining vs. post-training

*TODO: brief. Pretraining is where factual knowledge (and factual errors) come from. Post-training (RLHF, Constitutional AI, etc.) shapes tone, refusals, and format — it does not reliably fix factual gaps.*

## Why LLMs hallucinate

- They are trained to produce fluent, plausible text — not to abstain.
- The training objective rewards confident completion, not calibrated uncertainty.
- For rare facts (a specific citation, a recent paper), the model interpolates.

## What this means for you

- Facts at the long tail of the training distribution (specific papers, recent tools, niche protocols) are the highest-risk outputs.
- Context you provide in the prompt is *qualitatively* more reliable than facts the model recalls.
- Retrieval (RAG), tool use, and grounding in real documents reduce — but do not eliminate — hallucination.

## Further reading

- *TODO.*
